prompt: Which muscle is the primary agonist for elbow flexion?
explanation: Anterior arm flexors bend the elbow; triceps extends it.

distractorExplanations.b: Triceps is the primary elbow extensor.

prompt: The axial skeleton primarily includes:

options.text: Skull, vertebral column, ribs, and sternum

explanation: Axial = central axis; appendicular = limbs and girdles.

prompt: Which energy pathway dominates a 40-minute steady jog?

options.text: ATP–PC exclusively for the entire 40 minutes
Only immediate phosphagen with no oxygen use
Anaerobic glycolysis as the sole pathway with zero aerobic contribution

explanation: Longer submaximal work relies primarily on aerobic metabolism.

distractorExplanations.b: Oxygen use rises in prolonged work.
distractorExplanations.d: Glycolysis contributes but is not sole for 40 min steady work.

prompt: A concentric muscle action occurs when:

options.text: Tension is produced with no joint motion (isometric)
The muscle is completely passive with zero tension
The muscle shortens while producing force

explanation: Concentric = shortening; eccentric = lengthening under tension; isometric = no length change.

distractorExplanations.c: Passive tissue is not an active concentric action.

prompt: Which chamber pumps oxygenated blood into the systemic circulation?

options.text: Left atrium only as the systemic pump

explanation: Left ventricle ejects to the aorta; right ventricle pumps to the lungs.

distractorExplanations.b: Right atrium receives systemic venous blood.
distractorExplanations.d: Left atrium receives pulmonary venous blood; ventricle pumps out.

prompt: Golgi tendon organs primarily respond to:

options.text: Tension / force in the tendon–muscle unit

explanation: GTOs sense tension and can inhibit agonist force (autogenic inhibition).

prompt: Which plane separates the body into anterior and posterior portions?
explanation: Frontal = front/back; sagittal = left/right; transverse = top/bottom.

distractorExplanations.d: Not a standard anatomical plane name.

prompt: Type II muscle fibers generally:

options.text: Produce higher force/power and fatigue faster than Type I

explanation: Fast-twitch fibers support power; Type I support endurance.

distractorExplanations.c: Skeletal muscle contains Type II fibers.

prompt: The rotator cuff muscles include:

options.text: Supraspinatus, infraspinatus, teres minor, and subscapularis
Rectus abdominis, obliques, transverse abdominis, erectors only
Vastus lateralis, medialis, intermedius, rectus femoris only

explanation: SITS muscles stabilize the glenohumeral joint.

prompt: Stroke volume is:

options.text: The amount of blood ejected by the ventricle per beat

explanation: Cardiac output = HR × SV.

prompt: Which joint type is freely movable with a synovial cavity?

options.text: Fibrous joint with no movement ever by definition of all fibrous joints

explanation: Synovial diarthroses move freely; sutures are immovable synarthroses.

distractorExplanations.d: Some fibrous joints allow minimal motion, but not synovial freedom.

prompt: Kyphosis refers to:

options.text: Excessive convex curvature of the thoracic spine (hunching)

explanation: Kyphosis = thoracic flexion exaggeration; lordosis = lumbar extension exaggeration; scoliosis = lateral.

prompt: Which connective tissue connects bone to bone?

options.text: Aponeurosis as identical to ligament always

explanation: Ligaments stabilize joints; tendons attach muscle to bone.

prompt: During inhalation at rest, the diaphragm primarily:

options.text: Relaxes and ascends as the only inhalation action
Contracts and descends, increasing thoracic volume

explanation: Diaphragm contraction expands the thorax; relaxation assists exhalation at rest.

distractorExplanations.c: Diaphragm is respiratory, not cardiac pump.
distractorExplanations.d: Diaphragm does not move the elbow.

prompt: The agonist–antagonist relationship for knee extension is primarily:

options.text: Hamstrings agonist for extension; quads antagonist
Only tibialis anterior as knee extensor

explanation: Quads extend the knee; hamstrings flex and can eccentrically control extension.

distractorExplanations.b: Calves act mainly at the ankle.

prompt: Progressive overload means:

options.text: Gradually increasing training stress so adaptation continues
Never changing load after week one

explanation: Overload can be load, volume, density, or complexity within recovery capacity.

distractorExplanations.d: Stretching alone isn’t full overload for strength.

prompt: A typical hypertrophy repetition range is approximately:

options.text: Only 1 rep at 100% every set forever
6–12 reps with challenging loads and sufficient volume
Only 50–100 reps with no load ever

explanation: Moderate reps and volume are classic hypertrophy guidelines.

distractorExplanations.a: Constant max singles are strength peaking.

prompt: Specificity (SAID) principle states that:

options.text: Adaptations match the demands imposed on the system

explanation: Train the quality and pattern you want to improve.

prompt: Rest intervals for near-maximal strength sets are commonly:

options.text: One full day between every set

explanation: Neural and phosphagen recovery needs longer rests for heavy loads.

prompt: Periodization organizes training by:

options.text: Planned variation of volume/intensity across cycles (micro/meso/macro)
Random workouts with no plan ever

explanation: Periodization manages fatigue and peaks performance.

distractorExplanations.b: Planning beats pure randomness for goals.

prompt: Compound lifts are valuable because they:

options.text: Train multiple joints/muscles and allow heavier systemic loading

explanation: Squats, hinges, presses, rows build efficient total-body strength.

prompt: FITT-VP variables include:

options.text: Frequency, Intensity, Time, Type, Volume, Progression

explanation: FITT-VP structures cardiorespiratory and conditioning prescriptions.

prompt: Plyometric training emphasizes:

options.text: Only static stretching holds of 10 minutes
Stretch-shortening cycle power and reactive ability

explanation: Plyometrics use rapid eccentric–concentric coupling when the client is prepared.

prompt: A safe general warm-up aims to:

options.text: Raise temperature and rehearse movement patterns without excessive fatigue
Exhaust the client completely before work sets
Skip entirely before heavy lifting always as best practice

explanation: Warm-ups prepare tissues and the nervous system.

prompt: Which cue best fits a hip hinge deadlift pattern?

options.text: Round the lumbar spine maximally under load as the goal
Push hips back, keep a neutral spine, and load the posterior chain
Squat only with vertical shins never hinging
Shrug the bar with arms only

explanation: Hinge = hip flexion/extension dominance with spinal bracing.

distractorExplanations.a: Loaded lumbar flexion is risky as a goal.
distractorExplanations.d: Arms are hooks, not prime movers.

prompt: Circuit training is often used to:

options.text: Improve conditioning and work capacity with limited rest between exercises
Guarantee hypertrophy better than any other method always

explanation: Circuits trade some max strength specificity for density/conditioning.

distractorExplanations.d: Hypertrophy depends on tension/volume, not circuits alone.

prompt: Tempo prescription 3-1-2-0 typically means:

options.text: 3s eccentric, 1s pause, 2s concentric, 0s top pause (example interpretation)

explanation: Tempos control time under tension and positions.

prompt: Detraining occurs when:

options.text: Training stimulus is removed long enough that adaptations reverse
Someone takes one easy deload day forever losing all fitness instantly always

explanation: Use-it-or-lose-it applies over weeks more than a single easy day.

prompt: Unilateral training can help:

options.text: Address side-to-side imbalances and challenge stability
Replace all bilateral work forever for powerlifters peaking

explanation: Split squats/rows complement bilateral lifts.

prompt: RPE (rate of perceived exertion) is used to:

options.text: Autoregulate intensity based on how hard the set feels
Replace all need for load tracking forever without notes

explanation: RPE complements percentages for day-to-day readiness.

distractorExplanations.d: Logging still helps even with RPE.

prompt: For apparently healthy older adults, resistance training is generally:

options.text: Recommended to preserve muscle, bone, and function with appropriate progressions
Limited to only finger movements forever

explanation: Age-appropriate strength training supports independence; screen and progress wisely.

distractorExplanations.b: Age alone is not a ban.

prompt: A pregnant client (uncomplicated) may often continue exercise with:

options.text: Physician guidance, avoiding supine late-pregnancy risks as advised, and monitoring warning signs
Contact sports collisions as preferred conditioning

explanation: Prenatal exercise is often beneficial with medical clearance and modifications.

prompt: Youth training should emphasize:

options.text: Maximal lifting to failure daily for 8-year-olds as standard
Technique, fun, gradual progressions, and age-appropriate loads

explanation: Skill and habits first; supervise closely.

prompt: Clients with hypertension (controlled, cleared) should generally:

options.text: Perform only max isometric holds breath-holding as the plan
Use stimulants liberally pre-workout despite advice
Favor steady breathing, avoid prolonged maximal Valsalva, and progress aerobically/resistance as cleared

explanation: BP management and breathing strategies matter; follow clinician guidance.

prompt: For obese clients new to exercise, a practical start is:

options.text: Low-impact cardio and foundational resistance with joint-friendly progressions
Plyometric depth jumps on day one for everyone
Only fasting without movement as training

explanation: Reduce joint stress and build adherence first.

prompt: A functional movement screen / posture assessment helps trainers:

options.text: Identify movement limitations and compensation patterns to guide programming

explanation: Screens inform training decisions; they are not medical diagnosis.

prompt: Clients with osteoporosis (cleared) should often avoid:

options.text: High-risk spinal flexion/loaded twisting movements that elevate fracture risk (individualize with clinician input)

explanation: Emphasize safe loading and fall prevention; modify spinal stress.

prompt: Asthma clients may need:

options.text: Maximal cold dry sprints without prep as first session always
Extended warm-ups, trigger awareness, and inhaler access per their action plan

explanation: Coordinate with medical plans; avoid known triggers.

prompt: Post-rehab clients returning from physical therapy should:

options.text: Obtain clearance, respect precautions, and progress under appropriate communication with the care team when needed
Jump to advanced plyometrics against PT restrictions
Stop all medical follow-up as soon as gym access begins

explanation: Scope boundaries and gradual loading protect healing tissues.

prompt: Body composition assessment via skinfolds estimates:

options.text: Subcutaneous fat to estimate body fat percentage using equations

explanation: Skinfolds are field estimates with technician error; interpret cautiously.

prompt: Clients with Type 2 diabetes (cleared) should be taught to:

options.text: Inject insulin doses decided by the trainer
Monitor for hypo/hyperglycemia signs around exercise and coordinate with their care plan
Use exercise to replace all prescribed medication unilaterally

explanation: Trainers support safe activity; medication changes are medical.

prompt: Balance training for fall-prone older adults should progress from:

options.text: Bosu single-leg eyes-closed with loads on day one for frail clients always
Stable bilateral support → narrower bases → perturbations as safe

explanation: Progress challenge gradually with spotting/support.

prompt: PAR-Q / health screening is used to:

options.text: Identify risk factors needing clearance or modification before exercise

explanation: Screening reduces adverse-event risk and guides referral.

prompt: For clients with low back pain history (cleared, non-acute), programming often emphasizes:

options.text: Neutral-spine bracing, hip hinging competency, and gradual exposure
Complete bed rest forever as the only option
Ignoring pain red flags like saddle anesthesia

explanation: Movement quality and load management beat aggressive spinal flexion contests; red flags need medical care.

distractorExplanations.a: High-rep loaded flexion may aggravate some clients.

prompt: Cardiac rehabilitation phase exercise for post-event clients is typically:

options.text: Unmanaged maximal HIIT on day one post-MI without clearance
Physician-directed with monitoring—trainers must stay in scope and follow clearance

explanation: Post-cardiac clients need medical oversight; CPT roles are supportive within clearance.

distractorExplanations.a: Clearance and progression rules are mandatory.

prompt: Within ISSA/CPT scope, nutrition guidance should be:

options.text: General healthy guidelines and referral to RDs for clinical diets
Medical nutrition therapy for renal failure prescribed by the trainer
Diagnosing nutrient deficiencies from a glance
Stopping physician-prescribed meds for weight loss

explanation: Stay in scope; disease-specific diets need licensed clinicians.

prompt: Which macronutrient provides the most kcal per gram?
explanation: Fat ≈9 kcal/g; carb/protein ≈4; alcohol ≈7; water 0.

prompt: Adequate protein supports training by:

options.text: Replacing all need for resistance training
Acting as the only fuel in phosphagen metabolism
Providing amino acids for repair and remodeling of muscle tissue

explanation: Protein enables adaptation; training stimulus and recovery still required.

prompt: Behavior change is more likely when goals are:

options.text: Specific, measurable, and supported by environmental cues/habits

explanation: Implementation intentions and monitoring improve adherence.

prompt: Client confidentiality requires:

options.text: Protecting personal health and training information from unauthorized disclosure
Sharing charts with friends for fun
Selling email lists of medical histories

explanation: Privacy builds trust and meets ethical/legal expectations.

prompt: A professional CPT business practice includes:

options.text: Guaranteeing 30 lb fat loss in 10 days for every client
Clear contracts, truthful marketing, and documented scope boundaries
Training without CPR/AED readiness where required

explanation: Integrity and safety systems reduce liability and build reputation.

prompt: Motivational interviewing spirit includes:
explanation: MI elicits the client’s own reasons for change.

prompt: Dehydration can impair performance by:

options.text: Removing the need to drink during long hot sessions
Increasing cardiovascular strain and reducing heat tolerance

explanation: Fluid deficits raise HR and heat illness risk.

prompt: Supplements marketed with disease-cure claims should prompt a trainer to:

options.text: Avoid disease-treatment claims and refer clients to qualified clinicians; stay evidence-cautious
Inject clients in the locker room
Guarantee FDA drug approval for all powders
Diagnose disease to sell more bottles

explanation: Supplements are not substitutes for medical care; scope limits apply.

prompt: Which is an open-ended coaching question?

options.text: What made it easier to hit your steps on the days you succeeded?
Yes or no: you hate vegetables?

explanation: Open questions explore success factors and barriers.

prompt: Sleep restriction chronically tends to:

options.text: Increase deep recovery beyond normal sleep
Impair recovery, hormone regulation, and training performance

explanation: Sleep is a primary recovery pillar.

distractorExplanations.a: Sleep loss is not a periodization strategy.

prompt: A client wants a meal plan for diagnosed celiac disease. The CPT should:

options.text: Design a clinical gluten-free medical protocol as MNT
Refer to an RD/physician for medical nutrition therapy and avoid practicing dietetics beyond scope

explanation: Celiac MNT is clinical; trainers can support general adherence habits after professional guidance.

distractorExplanations.a: Clinical MNT is out of scope.

prompt: Liability risk decreases when trainers:

options.text: Screen, document, obtain informed consent, and refer red flags
Ignore chest pain to finish a session package

explanation: Risk management is proactive and ethical.

prompt: NEAT (non-exercise activity thermogenesis) refers to:

options.text: Only resting metabolic rate in a coma
Calories burned in daily movement outside formal exercise

explanation: Steps, standing, and chores contribute meaningfully to total expenditure.

prompt: Ethical advertising for training services should avoid:

options.text: Honest descriptions of methods and expected process
Before/after photo manipulation and unverifiable miracle claims

explanation: Truthful marketing protects consumers and the profession.
